attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department following a motor vehicle accident. He has severe abdominal and chest pain. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears anxious. His pulse rate is 118/min, blood pressure 86/68 mmHg, respiratory rate 25/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. There is a scalp laceration that has stopped bleeding. His trachea is midline and bilateral breath sounds are heard on chest auscultation. Heart sounds are normal with no murmurs. Jugular veins are collapsed. Abdominal examination reveals some tenderness to both upper quadrants, but no rigidity, guarding, or rebound tenderness. There is no obvious bruising. The rest of the examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
options: CT Trauma Panscan (brain, neck, chest, abdomen, pelvis)
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with signs suggestive of blunt abdominal trauma (BAT) following a motor vehicle accident. BAT is often associated with injury to solid organs such as the liver or spleen, which can lead to intra-abdominal haemorrhage and hypovolaemic shock. The management of patients with suspected BAT is based on their haemodynamic status and signs of peritonitis.

In this case, the patient is haemodynamically unstable (tachycardic and hypotensive) but without signs of peritonitis. According to trauma management guidelines, the most appropriate next step is a bedside FAST ultrasound (Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma). FAST is used to detect the presence of intra-abdominal fluid, which in this context would likely indicate haemorrhage. If intra-abdominal fluid is detected in a haemodynamically unstable patient, it is an indication for emergency laparotomy.

CT abdomen is indicated in haemodynamically stable patients with high suspicion of intra-abdominal injury. While it provides detailed information about specific organ injuries, it is time-consuming and requires moving the patient out of the resuscitation area, which is inappropriate for an unstable patient.

CT neck is typically used for radiographic clearance of the cervical spine in patients with high-risk mechanisms of injury who have neurologic deficits or altered mental status. This patient does not exhibit these symptoms, and addressing potential intra-abdominal haemorrhage is more urgent.

A CT Trauma Panscan is a comprehensive imaging study that includes the brain, neck, chest, abdomen, and pelvis. While it provides a thorough evaluation, it is not appropriate as the next step for an unstable patient, as it is time-consuming and requires moving the patient away from immediate resuscitation capabilities.

An X-ray abdomen has limited utility in this scenario. It may be used when there's suspicion of gastrointestinal tract perforation, which typically presents with signs of peritonitis. This patient does not show these signs, and an X-ray would not provide information about potential solid organ injury or intra-abdominal haemorrhage.

FAST ultrasound has become increasingly important in the early assessment of trauma patients in Emergency Departments. It is immediately available, non-invasive, and can be repeated as needed. When performed by trained operators, it is highly accurate for detecting intra-abdominal free fluid. In this case, with a blunt force mechanism, abdominal pain, and hypotension, a FAST scan is the most appropriate next step to quickly assess for intra-abdominal haemorrhage and guide further management.
learningPoints: Bedside ultrasound examination is key in the initial assessment of a patient with suspected intra-abdominal injuries after a major accident.
FAST ultrasound is the most appropriate next step for evaluating haemodynamically unstable patients with suspected blunt abdominal trauma.
CT imaging is reserved for haemodynamically stable patients with suspected intra-abdominal injury.
references: Richards J, Schleper N, Woo B, Bohnen P, McGahan J. Sonographic assessment of blunt abdominal trauma: A 4-year prospective study. Journal of Clinical Ultrasound. 2002;30(2):59-67. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11857510/
Brown MA, Casola G, Sirlin CB, Patel NY, Hoyt DB. Blunt abdominal trauma: screening us in 2,693 patients. Radiology. 2001 Feb;218(2):352-8. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11161146/

Quinn AC, Sinert R. What is the utility of the Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma (FAST) exam in penetrating torso trauma? Injury. 2011 May;42(5):482-7. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20701908/
